items.q: Why is the Demographic Transition Model criticised for not applying to all countries?
items.correct: It was based on European industrialisation. LICs today face different conditions: imported medicine without industrialisation, cultural factors, gender inequality and globalised economies — not all follow the same path.
items.misconception: All countries follow the five stages of the DTM in order.
items.random_wrong: The DTM only applies to countries with more than 50 million people.
items.why: Many LICs experienced rapid death rate falls without industrialisation, breaking the model's logic. Some HICs are entering a Stage 5 it didn't predict — the DTM is a starting point, not a universal law.

items.q: What happens in Stage 2 of the Demographic Transition Model?
items.correct: Death rates fall rapidly (due to better food, sanitation and medicine) while birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth.
items.misconception: Birth rates rise rapidly because people want larger families.
items.random_wrong: Both birth and death rates fall sharply in Stage 2.
items.why: The population explosion in Stage 2 is driven by falling deaths, not rising births. Students see growth and assume more babies are being born when in fact more babies are surviving.

items.q: What is the main criticism of Rostow's stages of economic growth?
items.correct: It assumes all countries can follow the same linear Western industrialisation path, ignoring colonial legacies, global trade inequalities and environmental constraints — a top-down modernisation view.
items.misconception: All LICs will pass through Rostow's five stages if they wait long enough.
items.random_wrong: Rostow's model is universally accepted as the best development theory.
items.why: Rostow assumed Western industrialisation as a template, but global economic structures may prevent LICs replicating it. Dependency theorists argue underdevelopment is actively produced — not just a delay.

items.q: Replacement level total fertility rate is:
items.why: Correct answer: 2.1. “1.1” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: 1929 Warren Thompson. “1789 Malthus” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 1.49. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
